svg.text: Dependencies managed by AWS · every module ships as the same kind of container · AI behind one gateway, with a path to our own models
1 · THE GO-LIVE SHAPE FOR MAPLE ENTERPRISE (PHASE 2) — apps on one box, everything precious managed by AWS
EC2 — one rented computer, running Docker Compose
Caddy — HTTPS + routes each subdomain to its container
MapleOne suite apps — one container each
modules flip on/off per plan (flipt flags)
Apps are disposable: box dies → new box + `docker compose up` → back in ~30 min with zero data loss,
because nothing precious lives here — data in RDS, files in S3, secrets in Secrets Manager, images in ECR.
today's models — Step A of the AI track (panel 3)
test → build → push to ECR (right) → box pulls
MANAGED BY AWS — the precious parts
RDS PostgreSQL — auto backups, restore button

g.text: one instance, one DB per module:

svg.text: logs · CPU/disk/DB alarms · site-down alert to your phone
+ daily AI-spend report from the gateway
every module → its own database on RDS
2 · GROW IN PHASES — each triggered by a real pain, never skipped

g.text: Leave when: a real client signs —
box dying becomes a business problem
Panel 1 above: apps stay on the box;
Good for: Maple Enterprise + first pilots
Leave when: workloads contend for the
ECS Fargate: AWS runs each module's
containers directly — no server to patch.
ALB routes by hostname. Modules scale,

svg.text: Rollout order: box live → AI gateway → RDS → S3 → Secrets → ECR → restore drill → RBAC fixes → onboard the client → Fargate later.
3 · THE AI TRACK — one gateway, three steps, modules never notice the switch

g.text: maple-ai service: holds all keys, routes to
≈ one week — mostly moving existing code
Same foundation models, served by AWS
inside our account — no third-party keys,
a gateway config change; modules untouched
Open models tuned on our data, served on
SageMaker or GPU + vLLM. Route per use-case:

svg.text: THE FINE-TUNING LOOP (the moat — starts collecting value on day one)

g.text: route only if it beats incumbent

svg.text: Honest economics: at today's volume the APIs win by a mile (~₹8–10/page). The gateway's spend log is the evidence for when that flips.

g.text: □ own database on the shared RDS (own migrations)
□ own AUTH_SECRET standalone · shared secret = suite SSO
□ config from env/secrets only — nothing baked in
□ files on S3 via the storage lib, never local disk
□ cross-module talk: OutboxEvents + REST, never imports
□ AI only via the maple-ai gateway

svg.text: Meets the contract → gets a subdomain, a DB, a pipeline, a dashboard
row — in an afternoon. quotations + photoshoot already fit ~90%.
5 · KEEPING IT ALIVE — the honest weekly reality

g.text: Backups — RDS daily automatic + S3 versioning + monthly pg_dump.
Quarterly restore drill: an untested backup is a rumour. (~1h/quarter)
Monitoring — UptimeRobot on every public domain → phone;
CloudWatch alarms on CPU / disk / DB / 5xx; daily AI ₹ report. (setup once)
Updates — merge to main → Actions deploys; develop → stage first;
OS patches monthly (Fargate later = nobody patches). (~1h/month)
Security — DB/S3 private, SSH by key, MFA on root; fix the known
RBAC gaps (roles-API escalation) BEFORE any external customer.
Costs — billing alarm at 2× expected; 10-min monthly review of the
gateway spend log. Rotate secrets every 6 months.
Total steady-state effort: a few hours a month — by design.
· Phase 2 ≈ one decent office chair per month — buy it when a client signs
· Never buy GPUs to feel serious; buy them when the spend log says so
· Per-customer instances make pricing easy: their stack, their bill
Billing alarm at 2× expected — surprises come from typos, not traffic.

svg.text: Read with: docs/architecture/aws-deployment.md (the full written plan) · er-platform.md (proposed platform + AI tables) · er-suite.md / er-quotations.md (app data) ·
event-catalog.md (module integration) · rbac-matrix.md (access gaps to fix pre-launch). Companion click-by-click runbook: AWS-RUNBOOK-PHASE2.md.
The test of this architecture: onboarding customer #4 should be boring.
